did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses DermaNetX, a hybrid deep learning framework combining ResNet-50 and EfficientNet-B4 via dual-branch transfer learning, to perform automated multi-class skin disease classification on the HAM10000 dermoscopic image dataset. It employs weighted cross-entropy loss, data augmentation, and Grad-CAM for explainability to obtain results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper introduces DermaNetX, a novel hybrid deep learning framework, and presents original experimental results including training methodology, dataset composition (HAM10000), performance metrics (balanced accuracy 79.45%, macro AUC 0.9518), and ablation studies comparing it to single-backbone baselines. It is not a review or survey.

title: DermaNetX: An Efficient Deep Learning Framework For Automated Skin Disease Classification
journal: AMERICAN JOURNAL OF AI CYBER COMPUTING MANAGEMENT
brief_summary: This study introduces DermaNetX, a hybrid deep learning framework for automated multi-class skin disease classification that combines ResNet-50 and EfficientNet-B4 via dual-branch transfer learning. The model was trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermoscopic images across seven disease categories, utilizing weighted cross-entropy loss and data augmentation to address severe class imbalance. Evaluation on a held-out test set demonstrated a balanced accuracy of 79.45% and a macro-averaged AUC of 0.9518. Key results included high recall rates for critical classes, specifically 80% for melanoma and 94% for dermatofibroma. Ablation studies confirmed that the hybrid architecture outperformed single-backbone baselines in all evaluation metrics. Additionally, Grad-CAM was employed to provide spatial explainability, verifying that the model focuses on clinically relevant lesion features.

lesion_types_studied.details: Pre-malignant class in HAM10000 dataset; 327 samples; precision 0.63, recall 0.78, F1 0.70.
Malignant class in HAM10000 dataset; 514 samples; precision 0.62, recall 0.79, F1 0.69.
Benign class in HAM10000 dataset; 1,099 samples; precision 0.51, recall 0.81, F1 0.63.
Benign class in HAM10000 dataset; 115 samples; precision 0.48, recall 0.94, F1 0.64; noted as rarest class with high recall.
Malignant class in HAM10000 dataset; 1,113 samples; precision 0.28, recall 0.80, F1 0.42; critical metric for screening.
Benign class in HAM10000 dataset; 6,705 samples (67% of dataset); precision 0.99, recall 0.53, F1 0.69; dominant class causing imbalance.
Benign/Rare class in HAM10000 dataset; 142 samples; precision 0.65, recall 0.91, F1 0.75; highest individual AUC (0.997).

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin diseases (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic images using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete disease labels via a three-layer classification head with weighted cross-entropy loss. Performance is evaluated using balanced accuracy (79.45%), macro AUC (0.9518), per-class precision, recall, and F1-scores.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images used as the primary benchmark for the study. It contains 10,015 dermoscopic images across seven diagnostic categories, characterized by severe class imbalance (e.g., Melanocytic Nevi at 67% vs Dermatofibroma at 1.1%).

use.training: Used for training the DermaNetX hybrid framework via a two-phase transfer learning approach (Phase 1: frozen backbone pre-training; Phase 2: selective fine-tuning of final two convolutional blocks). Data augmentation and WeightedRandomSampler were applied.
use.validation: Used for validation during training to monitor loss and trigger early stopping (patience=10 in Phase 2).
use.testing: Used as a held-out test set to evaluate final performance metrics including balanced accuracy (79.45%), macro AUC (0.9518), and per-class precision/recall/F1.
use.other: Used for ablation studies comparing DermaNetX against single-backbone baselines (ResNet-50 and EfficientNet-B4) and for Grad-CAM explainability analysis.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address severe class imbalance by assigning sample weights inversely proportional to class frequency during training.
To mitigate class imbalance by applying class-specific weights (e.g., dermatofibroma weight 2.689, vascular lesion 2.173, melanocytic nevi 0.046) to the loss function.
To split the dataset into training (70%), validation (15%), and test (15%) sets while preserving class distribution.
To evaluate model performance by equally weighting all classes, serving as the primary metric for comparison.
To assess one-vs-rest discrimination capability across all disease classes.
To evaluate probabilistic discrimination for individual classes (e.g., Vascular Lesion AUC 0.997, Melanoma AUC 0.910).
To compare the hybrid DermaNetX architecture against single-backbone baselines (ResNet-50 and EfficientNet-B4) to verify performance gains.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (balanced accuracy, macro AUC, precision, recall, F1-score) and ablation studies without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: DermaNetX outperformed single-backbone baselines in all evaluation metrics. The model achieved high recall for rare classes (Dermatofibroma 94%, Vascular Lesion 91%) and Melanoma (80%). All seven classes exceeded an AUC of 0.90. The balanced accuracy of 79.45% was the primary metric. Overall accuracy was 63.0%, dominated by the Melanocytic Nevi class.

architecture_used.main_model: DermaNetX (Hybrid Dual-Branch CNN combining ResNet-50 and EfficientNet-B4)
architecture_used.pretraining: Both ResNet-50 and EfficientNet-B4 backbones were pre-trained on ImageNet.

architecture_details.backbone_branch_a: ResNet-50 (ImageNet-pretrained), last fully connected layer removed, outputting a 2048-dim feature vector via global average pooling.
architecture_details.backbone_branch_b: EfficientNet-B4 (ImageNet-pretrained), classifier head removed, outputting a 1792-dim feature vector using compound scaling for multi-scale spatial features.
architecture_details.fusion_method: Concatenation of the 2048-dim and 1792-dim vectors to form a 3840-dim fused feature vector.
architecture_details.classification_head: Three-layer feed-forward network: Linear(3840 → 512) → BatchNorm → ReLU → Dropout(0.4); Linear(512 → 256) → BatchNorm → ReLU → Dropout(0.3); Linear(256 → 7) (7-class output).

training_details.strategy: Two-phase transfer learning with selective fine-tuning.

phase_2.description: Selective fine-tuning of the last two convolutional blocks of each backbone.

training_details.loss_function: Weighted Cross-Entropy Loss (weights: dermatofibroma 2.689, vascular lesion 2.173, melanocytic nevi 0.046).

classification_or_output_head.type: 3-layer MLP with Batch Normalization and Dropout

preprocessing_techniques: Stratified random sampling for dataset splitting (70% training, 15% validation, 15% test)

Data augmentation applied only to training set: random resized crop (scale 0.8–1.0), horizontal flip (p=0.5), vertical flip (p=0.5), rotation ±30°, color jitter (brightness, contrast, saturation, hue ±0.2), Gaussian blur (p=0.3)
WeightedRandomSampler for handling class imbalance during training

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Brightness, contrast, saturation, and hue adjusted by ±0.2

conclusions: The proposed DermaNetX hybrid framework, combining ResNet-50 and EfficientNet-B4 via dual-branch transfer learning, achieved a balanced accuracy of 79.45% and a macro-averaged AUC of 0.9518 on the HAM10000 dataset.

DermaNetX outperformed single-backbone baselines (ResNet-50 and EfficientNet-B4) in all evaluation metrics, with performance gains attributed to the fusion of compound-scaled multi-resolution features and residual skip connections.
The model effectively addressed severe class imbalance, achieving high recall rates for critical and rare classes: 80% for melanoma, 94% for dermatofibroma, and 91% for vascular lesion.
Grad-CAM spatial explainability verified that the model focuses on clinically relevant lesion features, such as irregular edges and asymmetric pigment patterns, facilitating clinical auditing.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding the visual similarity between melanoma and melanocytic nevi, which presents an inherent challenge for classification despite high AUC scores.

Future work includes expanding the dataset with ISIC 2020 data, improving confidence calibration via temperature scaling, incorporating patient metadata, and conducting prospective clinical validation.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits severe class imbalance (Melanocytic Nevi at 67% vs. Dermatofibroma at 1.1%), potentially limiting generalizability to balanced clinical populations.

No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on a held-out subset of the training dataset (HAM10000), raising concerns about data leakage risks and overfitting to specific image sources.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for performance comparisons between DermaNetX and baselines, relying instead on descriptive metrics.
Melanoma precision is notably low (0.28) due to a deliberate bias toward sensitivity (80% recall) for malignant classes, which may result in high false-positive rates in clinical screening.

Baseline performance for ResNet-50 and EfficientNet-B4 is reported as approximate ranges (~74–78% and ~76–80% balanced accuracy, respectively) rather than exact values, making precise comparison difficult.
The framework has not undergone prospective clinical validation or deployment in real-world settings; future work includes edge-capable hardware deployment and structured dermatologist evaluation.
Grad-CAM explainability was applied only to the ResNet-50 branch, not the full hybrid model, which may limit the completeness of the interpretability analysis.
